---
title: "How It Works"
description: "Understand the template build process and snapshot model behind E2B sandboxes."
---

# How It Works

When you build a sandbox template, E2B creates a container from the template definition, provisions it, runs the template steps, and then snapshots the resulting sandbox state.

In broad terms:

1. E2B starts from the selected base image or base template.
2. Builder steps such as `Copy(...)`, `RunCmd(...)`, and `SetEnvs(...)` are applied.
3. If you configured a start command, E2B runs it inside the build sandbox.
4. E2B waits for readiness. Without a custom ready command, the default is effectively immediate unless the Dockerfile parser supplied `WaitForTimeout(20_000)` for `CMD` or `ENTRYPOINT`.
5. E2B snapshots the sandbox so future `Create(...)` calls can boot from that captured state.

That snapshot is what E2B refers to as a template.

## Snapshot model

A template snapshot captures the sandbox filesystem together with the running processes needed for the template state.

This is what allows later sandboxes to start from an already-prepared environment rather than rebuilding the template each time.

## Default user and workdir

Templates default to the `user` account and `/home/user` working directory. For the Go SDK behavior and override patterns, see [User and Workdir](/docs/template/user-and-workdir).

## Caching

Template builds reuse matching layers and uploaded copy sources when possible. For the Go cache controls, see [Caching](/docs/template/caching).

## Kernel

E2B sandboxes run on an LTS 6.1 Linux kernel. The exact kernel version available inside a sandbox depends on when the template was built:

| Template build date | Kernel version |
| --- | --- |
| On or after 2025-11-27 | 6.1.158 |
| Before 2025-11-27 | 6.1.102 |

Kernel versions are fixed at template build time. To use a newer kernel, rebuild the template.

Kernel configuration files are published in the `e2b-dev/fc-kernels` repository.
